title: The Only Place That Would Rent to Jews
summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer in Arlington, Virginia, on St. Patrick's Day 2013, Joan Trumpauer Mulholland describes being born in Washington and raised in the Buckingham apartments, known as the only place in northern Virginia that would rent to Jews, among New York families chasing government jobs, with a father from southwest Iowa at the Department of Labor and a mother from rural Georgia.

title: Hypocrites as Soon as We Left Church
topic: A Literalist in a Segregated Church
summary: She recalls memorizing Bible verses for gold stars at the Presbyterian church, singing that Jesus loves the children red and yellow black and white while the church itself was segregated by law, and the secret Sunday-evening youth meetings where Black students from the YWCA came to share spaghetti dinners, kept quiet because the American Nazi Party headquarters was around the corner.

title: Lynching Was Not Breaking News
summary: Mulholland says she was barely aware of Emmett Till's 1955 murder, contrasting it with the Leo Frank lynching debated at her grandmother's in Oconee, Georgia, where she spent two weeks each summer, and recalls walking with another girl through the Black part of town and seeing with her own eyes the gap between the new brick white school and the Black school.

title: Those Yankees Looked Down Their Nose at Us
summary: Asked why white Southerners like her, Bob Zellner, and Casey Hayden joined SNCC and CORE before Northerners, she answers that Northerners looked down on them while Southerners were working on what they saw as their own community's problems, and recounts choosing Duke University because her status-minded mother wanted a name school that was safely segregated.

title: We Didn't Want to Rush
summary: Mulholland describes Duke in 1959 as dripping ivy and all about status, and how she and her New York roommate Lucia refused to rush a sorority, choosing a graduate-student potluck instead, prompting counselors to check whether they were unhappy because it had supposedly never happened in the university's history.

title: We Would All Be Others
summary: As a freshman she joined the Durham sit-ins, picketing and sitting at lunch counters at a Kress or Woolworth and going to jail twice, and tells how the Black students, finding the white students had a harder time inside, hatched a plan to exploit a North Carolina law providing separate cells for white, colored, and others by all claiming to be others.

title: She Put the Key in Her Pocket
summary: Mulholland recounts the dean locking her and Lucia in a dark administrative building and pocketing the key until they phoned their parents, the administration's wish to expel them blocked by the professors' organization, an English teacher offering to bring a pop quiz to the jail, her mother's view that she had joined a cult, and her father's fear his daughter would be killed.

part: Howard and the Nonviolent Action Group
title: Go Up to Howard and Get Something Going
summary: After dropping out and moving to Washington to work menial Senate office jobs, she joined the Howard-based Nonviolent Action Group on the advice of North Carolina College students who had not heard from Howard since SNCC's founding, wandering the campus asking after the sit-in students until she was welcomed into a group then planning to desegregate Arlington.

title: Waiting to Have Their Hand Forced
summary: Mulholland describes sitting in at Cherrydale and other Arlington lunch counters with about thirteen NAG members from Howard and the community, the American Nazi Party busing high schoolers up to confront them, and the locally owned chains like Drug Fair and Peoples, run by liberal New York Jewish owners, who after a week of demonstrations quietly opened to everyone.

title: Arrested on the Merry-Go-Round
summary: With the lunch counters won, NAG turned to Glen Echo Amusement Park, the segregated Disney of its day, where she bought tickets and handed them to Black students who rode the merry-go-round to be arrested, sparking a summer-long picket backed by the Jewish co-op community of Bannockburn against the American Nazi Party, whose swastikas unnerved World War II camp survivors.

part: Rock Hill and the Road to the Freedom Rides
title: Come Down and Join Them
summary: Mulholland tells of NAG as an on-call movement answering the call to Rock Hill, South Carolina, the farthest south she had been, where she was in contact with the adult contact Reverend C.A. Ivory, visited the jail-in, and was briefly taken into what she believes was protective custody rather than a real arrest.

title: The Whole Idea of the Freedom Ride Was Formed
topic: A Plan on a Greyhound Bus
summary: She traces how Diane Nash stayed her jail-in sentence and Tom Gaither of CORE, leaving Rock Hill, began on a Greyhound bus the idea of testing the Supreme Court's interstate-commerce ruling, how SNCC's April meeting in Charlotte called for students to test it, and how when the bus burned in Anniston three NAG men left at once while the Montgomery church came under siege.

title: Send More People
topic: The Call From the Besieged Church
summary: Mulholland recounts Paul calling from the basement of the surrounded Montgomery church to say send more people, NAG filtering riders in two or three at a time, the Kennedys' bargain with Mississippi that there would be no Alabama-style violence but riders could be arrested for breach of peace, and her own routine flight to New Orleans and train into Jackson.

title: He Jerked His Hand Back
summary: She describes the Jackson police captain instinctively reaching to help her down from the paddy wagon as a sweet young white thing, then jerking his hand back as if struck by lightning, a moment that gave her faith things would end well, and her famous calm mug shot, taken because unlike the Northern women she was at home in her own culture.

title: Less Than Three Square Feet Each
topic: Hinds County Jail and Women Power
summary: Mulholland recalls the overcrowded white women's cell in the county jail with people sleeping under bunks and in the dripping shower stall, feeling more in common with the Southern Black women in the next cell than the Yankee girls, and the support of Women Power Unlimited, the network of church women who supplied shower shoes, stamps, and toiletries.

title: Coming Out of the Woodwork Years Later
summary: She explains the thirty-nine-day window to bail before the six-month sentence sent people to Parchman, the homes that offered showers and clean clothes on release, the Women Power founder Clarie Collins Harvey who ran a funeral home, and meeting on a later cruise elderly Jackson churchwomen who had quietly insisted the riders were not violent communists.

title: The Full Horror Had Not Reached Virginia
summary: Mulholland describes the scripted two-month sentence and rising fines, the truck ride to the isolated cotton-plantation prison near where Emmett Till was murdered, the terror when the driver turned off the two-lane road toward a house, and arrival at Parchman where they were stripped, given striped skirts, and subjected to a vaginal exam one cellmate blocked out for decades.

title: They Cleaned Out Death Row
summary: She explains the segregated cells alternating white and Black down one cell block on death row, which was emptied for them and was cleaner with better food, how she knew people by voice without seeing faces, meeting Helen Singleton years later who remembered her voice, and the psychological cruelty of isolation where, unlike Hinds County, no reporters could hear them.

title: Lectures in Swahili Through the Walls
summary: Mulholland recounts the unremarkable Southern food, fried chicken and lemonade for a Fourth of July Mississippi barely celebrated since Vicksburg fell, taking up coffee, making playing cards from censored mail now in the American History Museum, and college-level lectures in Greek classics, Swahili, and French delivered cell to cell by women they could hear but not see.

title: Those Yankees Couldn't Sing
summary: She describes the cells smaller than Nelson Mandela's, mattresses taken away for singing, the Southern spiritual singing and clapping the Northern girls could not match, the once-weekly showers, and the local Black lawyers Jack H. Young Sr. and Carsie Hall who worked the cell block because outside counsel like William Kunstler was not recognized by Mississippi.

title: He Slipped In Little Bits of News
summary: Mulholland calls Rabbi Perry Nussbaum a hero who drove up faithfully each week from a Jackson Jewish community unwelcome to the power structure, praying in Hebrew to lull the wardens while slipping in world news and individual family messages, and notes that years later the synagogue and his home were bombed by the Klan.

title: Deserting the Rabbi for the Catholics
summary: She tells of choosing to pray with visiting Catholic seminarians over the rabbi and being scolded for deserting him, the lack of physical violence against the women though the men fared worse, her choice to serve the full sentence rather than bail since she had been accepted at Tougaloo, and getting out the week the rabbi left for a holy day.

title: School Integration Is a Two-Way Street
summary: Mulholland recounts coming out of Parchman to the Freedom House on Rose Street, then enrolling at Tougaloo College, which she had applied to in reaction to seeing Charlayne Hunter clutch her Madonna under riots at Georgia, believing integration had to run both ways, the school's pre-segregation charter and Northern funding making her presence possible.

title: A Ghost in a Flowery Shortie
summary: She describes being confused with the other white student Charlotte Phillips, a roommate startled to find a white roommate asleep on the bed, a girl screaming in the hall thinking she was a ghost in a pink flowery nightgown, and the social science forums and Ernst Borinski's basement lab that meant white visitors were not new, though a full-time white dorm resident was.

title: Students First and Foremost
summary: Mulholland explains that Tougaloo sat six miles north of downtown Jackson, that she did secretarial work at the SNCC and CORE office on Lynch Street near Medgar Evers but as a white woman did not go into the Delta, and that she ended up majoring in history rather than sociology to avoid three hours of statistics.

title: The Back Bedroom Was the Movement Room
summary: She recalls the campus NAACP youth chapter, Reverend Edwin King's back bedroom where Jeannette King kept coffee brewing as a movement room and where she typed legal documents as William Kunstler paced in his boxer shorts, and political science professor John R. Salter Jr., whose house was a headquarters for stuffing boycott envelopes.

title: Field Reports From the Delta
summary: Mulholland describes sitting next to Marlon Brando at Dean Branch's breakfast during the 1966 Meredith March, co-chairing the campus NAACP agitation group with Joyce Ladner, and compiling field reports mailed from Delta organizers into a stapled Mississippi newsletter, originals of which, including early references to Fannie Lou Hamer, she gave to the new Smithsonian museum.

title: I Was Going to Be a Spotter
summary: Mulholland recalls visiting Hodding Carter's office with Edwin King, then the spring 1963 escalation of the Jackson boycott, and a planned sit-in in which she and dorm mother Lois Chaffe were assigned as spotters for a picket-line diversion meant to draw police while demonstrators reached Woolworth, with dimes ready to phone Medgar Evers's office.

title: The Police Could Not Go In
topic: A Plan Unravels at the Counter
summary: She explains that the picket line was arrested at once instead of drawing police away, that a Supreme Court ruling the week before, in a consolidated case bearing her own name from Durham, barred police from entering a store uninvited, and that as a crowd of white Central High students gathered, Memphis Norman was pulled from his stool and stomped beside Anne Moody and Pearlena Lewis.

title: Blood Mixing With the Mustard
summary: Mulholland describes Edwin King suggesting she sit down at the counter for safety as someone brandished a knife, the police laughing outside while citing the Supreme Court ruling, John R. Salter Jr. taking the brunt with brass knuckles and being dubbed the mustard man, and Fred Blackwell capturing the iconic photograph from atop the counter.

title: Why Doesn't Salter Ever Turn Black
summary: She reflects that the Woolworth photograph, blown up at the Kennedy Library, has eclipsed the Greensboro image because violence feels imminent, and puzzles over children's-book illustrations that always render her figure as Black and female rather than John R. Salter Jr., suspecting it touches the old taboo of protecting white women from Black men.

title: Like a Guardian Angel Above Me
summary: Mulholland recounts the roughly three-hour ordeal that reporters called the most frightening of the movement, keeping conversation light by joking about Salter's hard exams, an out-of-body sense that the real her hovered above like a guardian angel, and the college president negotiating safe passage and even sitting at the counter once the store closed.

part: Medgar Evers and the March on Washington
title: Where Else Was I Going to Go
summary: She describes returning to the counter rather than the forming mob outside, being driven to the Black community near Medgar Evers's office and cleaned up at a beauty parlor, the packed mass meeting that night, Roy Wilkins and the national NAACP arriving, and Medgar Evers's assassination by Byron De La Beckwith with John Doar keeping the funeral from erupting into a riot.

title: It Had Been an Awful Year
summary: Mulholland recalls working the press section at the March on Washington headquarters amid fears the federal government would stop the buses, the awful year of Birmingham, the Letter From Birmingham Jail and King's dream speech, camping with Anne Moody on the way back, and the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing two weeks later that killed four girls.

part: Church Visits and Statements of Conscience
title: If You Can Make It to the Catholics
summary: She describes the church-visit campaign of integrated groups appealing to white congregations' conscience and often being arrested or turned away, knowing the downtown Catholic church offered sanctuary, Galloway Memorial Methodist as a major target where Joyce Ladner was arrested on the steps, and ministers like W.B. Selah who resigned and the signers of a statement of conscience forced from their pulpits.

part: Freedom Summer and the Canton Ambush
title: Do We Have the Right
summary: Mulholland recalls Bob Moses at the Freedom House on Rose Street agonizing over whether they had the right to bring students down knowing someone would be killed, and the night a carload of white activists drove to a mass meeting in Canton, a town off limits to whites under curfew, letting Hamid Kizilbash drive and finding themselves boxed in by men with crowbars on the new road.

title: He's From India, Don't Hit Him
topic: Almost Killed on the Canton Road
summary: She describes Edwin King shouting that the dazed and bleeding Hamid Kizilbash was a foreigner from India until the apparent leader, seemingly tipped off through diplomatic channels after an Indian opposition figure's arrest, pulled the attackers back, and her understanding that they were meant to die that night to stop Freedom Summer, so three others, Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney, died instead.
related_events: Murders of Chaney Goodman and Schwerner

title: Not a Bone in His Body Unbroken
summary: Working at the Smithsonian, Mulholland heard from Black workers that Chaney's body was brought in after dark with not a bone unbroken, a horror later confirmed when Dr. David Spain of the Medical Committee for Human Rights, at Fannie Lee Chaney's request, examined it and compared the injuries to an airplane crash, part of how Freedom Summer drew the world's attention.
related_events: Murders of Chaney Goodman and Schwerner

title: The First Printed Black Power
topic: Black Power at the Meredith March
summary: Mulholland recounts joining the 1966 Meredith March from Tougaloo carrying flyers folded into her New Testament that she believes were among the first printed references to Black Power, issued in Canton a week after the cry arose in Greenwood, soon echoed in a Washington Post political cartoon as the movement turned in a new direction.

title: He Knelt on the Floor for the Kids
summary: She describes bringing her freckled blonde twins, then about four, to meet Stokely Carmichael at a mid-1970s Smithsonian program despite friends' warnings, and how the towering and most feared man in white America knelt to talk eyeball to eyeball with them, a kindness she says counted more than the raised fist and that Joan Browning recalled too.

title: I Had to Do Something Right Raising Him
topic: Fifty Years and a Son's Film
summary: Mulholland closes on working menial jobs, marrying and raising five boys, three decades as a classroom teaching assistant bringing civil rights perspective, her continued speaking, and her son Loki Mulholland's documentary An Ordinary Hero about her life, as the interviewers thank her and the recording ends.
